title: UNH Upgraded by HSBC to Hold from Reduce, April 2026 - Meyka
content: HSBC upgraded UnitedHealth Group (UNH) from Reduce to Hold on April 14, 2026, signaling improved sentiment toward the healthcare giant. The upgrade reflects a shift in analyst perspective on the company’s near-term prospects. UnitedHealth trades at $314.19 with a market cap of $285.3 billion. The UNH upgrade comes as the stock shows resilience in the healthcare sector. This rating change matters for investors tracking healthcare insurance stocks and analyst consensus shifts. HSBC moved UnitedHealth from Reduce to Hold, marking a meaningful upgrade in analyst stance. The shift occurred on April 14, 2026, as market conditions and company fundamentals warranted reassessment. This upgrade reflects HSBC’s belief that downside risks have diminished. The stock was trading at $317.87 when the upgrade was published. Moderately bullish activity in UnitedHealth supported the rating change. The upgrade positions UNH more favorably among healthcare insurance peers. A Hold rating suggests HSBC sees limited upside or downside in the near term. Investors should not expect aggressive gains from this level. The rating implies the stock is fairly valued at current prices. Hold ratings typically indicate stability rather than momentum. This stance differs sharply from the previous Reduce rating, which warned of potential weakness. UnitedHealth trades at $314.19 with a $285.3 billion market cap, making it a healthcare sector heavyweight. The stock shows a P/E ratio of 23.75, reflecting moderate valuation relative to earnings. This consensus reflects confidence in UnitedHealth’s business model and market position. UnitedHealth operates in the Medical-Healthcare Plans industry within the broader Healthcare sector. The company manages four key segments: UnitedHealthcare, Optum Health, Optum Insight, and Optum Rx. These divisions span insurance, care delivery, software, and pharmacy services. title: Durham Town Hall: Student Senate shares Campus Carry stance and potential Cat’s Cache changes - The New Hampshire
content: DURHAM – Deeba Ajamian, a representative of the University of New Hampshire (UNH) Student Senate, shared the senate’s official stance on the Campus Carry Bill and hopes to improve UNH’s currency, Cat’s Cache, at the Durham Town Council meeting Monday, April 6. The current Cat’s Cache provider’s contract will end in two years. Ajamian, the external affairs chairperson of the UNH Student Senate, said that the senate was working on a resolution to encourage the university to change what provider handles Cat’s Cache. “Hopefully changing the [Cat’s Cache] provider will make it easier and more affordable for local businesses to have Cat’s Cache as an option,” said Ajamian. Ajamian noted the results of the Campus Carry Bill opinion survey the senate conducted in February influenced them to pass Resolution R.47.40 on April 12, opposing the bill. “A large majority of students are opposed to the campus carry bill,” said Ajamian. Resolution R.47.40 states that the senate’s campus carry opinion survey received 2361 responses, 85.9% of which said students would be less likely to attend UNH if the Campus Carry Bill were to pass. The resolution demands that the New Hampshire State Senate recognize UNH’s negative opinion of HB1793. It also urges lawmakers to mark it as “inexpedient to legislate,” which would kill the bill. A hearing for the Campus Carry Bill is set for April 14 at 1:45 p.m. in the New Hampshire state house in Concord.

title: NH college students protest bill to allow guns on campus - Seacoastonline.com
content: CONCORD — Students are speaking up on the future of gun-free college campuses in New Hampshire, while Gov. Kelly Ayotte has remained silent on the Republican-led bill that would allow students and others on campus to be armed. A protest against New Hampshire House Bill 1793 is being held April 14 outside the State House prior to a New Hampshire Senate Judiciary Committee public hearing on the proposed policy. Proponents of the bill are also expected to attend the hearing. Concealed and open carry are legal in New Hampshire without a license for adults, though all University System of New Hampshire schools are presently gun-free zones. If HB 1793, which passed the House previously, is passed by the state Senate and signed into law by Ayotte, UNH, Keene State, Plymouth State and other college campuses could not regulate firearms. The bill would bar public colleges and universities in New Hampshire from regulating firearm possession across campuses, in addition to non-lethal weapons such as pepper spray, mace, stun guns and tasers. The bill does not contain any provisions requiring safe storage of firearms. It has been opposed by a large majority of University of New Hampshire students, as well as UNH's administration, Durham town leaders and police, all of whom raise safety concerns. State Rep. Sam Farrington, R-Rochester, a current University of New Hampshire student, is the bill’s prime sponsor. Thirteen Republican lawmakers — nine from the House and four from the Senate — have co-sponsored the bill. Proponents like Farrington say they are hearing students want the right to be armed after incidents like the shooting at Brown University in late 2025, saying gun-free school zones don't lead to a violence-free zone. Ayotte's spokesperson had not replied as of mid-day April 14 to a request for comment on the governor's position on the bill. Students from the University of New Hampshire and Plymouth State University, law enforcement officials, UNH residence hall directors and anti-firearm advocates were among the speakers at the rally against HB 1793. Eli Orne, a UNH freshman, and Ronan Sangster, a Plymouth State University junior, represented their campuses at the protest. Orne pointed to mental health challenges in young adults and the risk of increased violence or deaths by suidide posed by HB 1793. “I believe that if people have unrestricted access to firearms, what could usually result in a hospitalization and be managed could become something that is fatal. That person would not be able to go on and live the rest of their lives,” Orne said. Sangster’s father is a career U.S. Marine who “has always ensured that I was familiar with gun safety, gun rights and the catastrophic effects that they can wreak on a human life,” Sangster said. The junior biology major worries if guns are allowed on campus students would not apply for residence hall leadership positions within the university system, increasing the risk for student assaults and mental health incidents to increase and go unreported. Sangster added the bill could lead to decreased enrollment across New Hampshire’s public colleges. “It will decrease educational opportunities for New Hampshire students who cannot afford to go to school out of state, and as such, limit access to housing for those in need. One way or another, this bill is going to kill people,” Sangster said. “It will put everyone involved at unnecessary risk in a place where they're already vulnerable and overall lead to an irreparable degradation of the quality of New Hampshire institutions.” Durham Police Department Deputy Chief Jack Dalton and Strafford County Sheriff Kathryn Mone, a former Durham police sergeant, received cheers for their opposition to HB 1793. Mone stated she received both her bachelor’s and graduate degrees on the UNH Durham campus. “Schools are for learning, not guns,” she said. “I was wearing a gun when I was working, and then I went to the classroom, where I was not wearing a gun. I was depending on the police who were armed and trained to come, if I called … and they were the ones that were going to come to the classroom and handle what needed to be handled, because I was there to learn.” The sheriff shared her belief that policies about firearms on public college campuses should be left to school administrators. According to Dalton, in Durham, 525 arrests have occurred in town since the start of UNH’s academic year. Among those charged, 285 are university students, and 359 arrests were alcohol-related. Guns should not be brought into the setting, Dalton argued. “This is not about rights,” he said. “This is about common sense, because now we’re going to add guns into an equation where we have young adults, 18 to 22 years old for the most part, learning to become young adults, learning to become good citizens. Now they’re going to have guns? We as a police department have sat back and said, ‘Well, how do we handle this?’” At a town hall in Durham in March, Dalton told attendees he would not allow his daughter, a high school senior, to enroll at UNH if HB 1793 becomes law. Farrington recently hosted a question-and-answer session on his bill on UNH’s Durham campus, where he studies. After the event, he said, two students messaged him to report he convinced them to support the measure. The first-term state representative brought forward the bill for “the safety concern and the constitutional right concern,” he stated prior to the April 14 public hearing. “I understand it’s emotional. I understand that some people don’t like guns, they don’t like being around guns. But what it comes down to is that this is a natural right protected by the Constitution,” Farrington said. In response to the Student Senate survey showing vast opposition to HB 1793, Farrington said, “It doesn’t change anything I do. I try to talk to the people that disagree with me.” He reported over 400 college students in New Hampshire signed a petition in support of the bill. “Preventing lawful students from carrying only ensures that potential victims are left defenseless against unlawful aggression,” the bill states. Fourteen states don’t outright ban concealed carry on their public university campuses, according to Everytown, a gun violence prevention organization: Arkansas, Georgia, Idaho, Kansas, Minnesota, Mississippi, Montana, South Dakota, Tennessee, Texas, Utah, West Virginia, Wisconsin and Wyoming. “It worries me because we live in a society where there are deranged people out there and criminals don’t have any regard for morality or what the law says,” Farrington said. “These gun control measures only restrict law-abiding, upstanding citizens.” A National Rifle Association lobbyist and state representative of the Oklahoma-based Women for Gun Rights were among the advocates of Farrington’s bill in Concord before the hearing. Farrington briefly congregated with younger pro-firearm advocates on the steps of the State House. “It’s a broad coalition,” he said of HB 1793’s supporters. “This bill seeks to allow anyone to carry a loaded firearm at any time, anywhere on campus,” said Zandra Rice Hawkins, director of GunSense NH and executive director of Engage NH. “We're talking not just classrooms, but dorms, large sporting venues, childcare centers that are on campus, contract events and more. This poses a significant danger to students, staff, visitors, to youth groups coming in the summer or on the weekends for youth tournaments or camps. This is not the kind of legislation we want to see in our state.” The brief rally was supported by numerous organizations: GunSense NH, Engage NH, NH Youth Movement, Moms Demand Action, MomsRising and the National Campaign to Keep Guns Off Campus. Thirty-eight seats and minimal standing room was available in the chamber ahead of the Senate Judiciary Committee hearing. Dozens of people began to sign up to testify on the bill roughly an hour before it began. Tim Hoheneder, a University of New Hampshire doctorate candidate in Durham, is the college’s student representative on the University System of New Hampshire’s board of trustees. Hoheneder’s peers were among those planning to testify before the Senate subcommittee and he appeared at the protest. He said UNH students taking the time to travel from Durham to Concord for the protest and hearing "really means a lot, not only to those of us who want to keep our campuses safe, but also to the institutional mission and institutional autonomy that USNH wants to keep over its campuses, as well." This story will be updated with more coverage from the events outside the State House. The use and possession of firearms is banned across the University of New Hampshire’s three campuses in Durham, Concord and Manchester. Written permission for “instructional or other qualified purposes and in other special circumstances and conditions” may be issued by the university’s police chief, per school policy. The university system reported its expenses in response to the bill could fall between $100,000 and $500,000 across its colleges, according to the bill’s fiscal note. Opponents of the bill have warned that could lead to further tuition hikes at USNH schools, which increased in-state tuition this academic year for the first time in six years. Schools within the university system could face lawsuits requesting a minimum of $10,000 in damages if HB 1793 becomes law. “Any individual aggrieved by a violation of this subdivision may bring an action against the public institution of higher education and its employees responsible for the violation and seek appropriate relief, including injunctive relief, monetary damages, reasonable attorneys' fees, and court costs,” the bill says. “Total damages awarded in an action brought under this subdivision shall be at least $10,000.” Daniel Gray, executive director of Citizens Alliance of New Hampshire, warned supporters of HB 1793 in an April 9 email that state senators may introduce amendments that would "significantly weaken” the bill. Gray called for the bill to be advanced as drafted. “This is our chance to expand our Second Amendment rights and give college students and faculty the ability to protect and defend themselves,” Gray’s message states. The University of New Hampshire’s Student Senate organized a survey regarding HB 1793, receiving 2,361 respondents from the Durham campus. Among them, 81.8% of students “strongly oppose” the bill, while another 3.5% are “somewhat opposed” to HB 1793, according to the Student Senate’s finalized report. In addition, 10.8% of student respondents strongly support HB 1793. Community town halls regarding the bill and its expected impacts have been held in Durham, Keene and Plymouth, home of the public schools within the University of New Hampshire System. House legislators advanced HB 1793 to the Republican-majority New Hampshire Senate in February after passing the bill in a 188-165 vote. The bill is now under review by the Senate Judiciary Committee. The policy would take effect Jan. 1, 2027, if signed into law.

title: UNH police chief resigns following DUI - Concord Monitor
content:
Sign up for the Concord Monitor’s morning newsletter for daily news, and opt in to our contests and promotions list for giveaways and special offers. The Concord Monitor is a Pulitzer Prize winning daily newspaper and website located in the capital city of New Hampshire. The Concord Monitor is Central NH's dominant media with more than 70 percent household penetration. The Monitor is a key source of news and information for Concord, NH. Sign up for the Concord Monitor’s morning newsletter for essential news each day, and our contests and promotions list for special offers and giveaways. University of New Hampshire Police Chief Steven Lee has resigned, two months after being arrested for driving under the influence in Portsmouth. Lee’s resignation was effective April 1, according to Tania deLuzuriaga, a university spokesperson. His decision to resign came after the university notified him that it intended to terminate him, according to a separation agreement obtained through a right-to-know request. “He believes that his transition from the university is in the Department’s best interest given the unfortunate event that occurred,” Lee’s attorney, Corey MacDonald, wrote in a statement to the Monitor. Lee, 50, was arrested in the early morning hours of Jan. 22 after an officer spotted his SUV swerving and nearly colliding with an 18-wheeler on Market Street in Portsmouth, according to a police report the Monitor obtained through a right-to-know request. Lee admitted to drinking “downtown” and said he was heading home, according to the report. The officer said Lee smelled of alcohol and noticed he had red, glossy eyes. He partially failed a series of field sobriety tests and declined to take a breathalyzer test. Award-winning coverage on breaking news and today’s top stories in and around the Capital Region. From Arts and Life to Sports and Opinion you’ll have unlimited access to the Concord Monitor's content. He had been on administrative leave since his arrest. In February, he pleaded guilty to the DUI charge. “Steve has taken the high road at every opportunity throughout this unfortunate process, insisting on full accountability and transparency,” MacDonald wrote. “He has acknowledged his mistake, and he has faced consequences that go far and away beyond what usually occurs when there is a DUI conviction.” Lee was named UNH’s police chief last year, following the departure of his predecessor, Paul Dean, who took a job in Gov. Kelly Ayotte’s administration. Lee worked full-time for the department for nearly two decades. As a condition of his plea, Lee’s driver’s license was suspended for nine months, with the possibility of earlier reinstatement. He was required to pay a $620 fine and participate in an impaired driver care management program, according to court records. MacDonald previously said that Lee placed himself on administrative leave and “proactively entered treatment catering to first responders.” “He is hopeful as he looks forward to the next chapter of his professional life, and he considers himself fortunate to have spent 30 years in public service doing good for his community,” MacDonald wrote. “Steve encourages other first responders to not take their own health and well-being lightly, and to lean into each other for guidance and support early on in their career.” In a letter accepting Lee’s resignation that was appended to his separation agreement, an unnamed university official thanked Lee for his “sustained dedication” to the university, but noted that it “does not diminish the gravity of what occurred earlier this year.” Former Captain Mark Collopy was appointed interim chief of UNH’s department following Lee’s arrest. He continues to serve in that role, according to deLuzuriaga. The university has yet to launch a search to fill the role permanently. The chief of police reports to the university’s vice president for administration and finance, Robert Watling, who started in that position within the past month, deLuzuriaga said. Editor’s note: This story has been updated with details from the separation agreement between Steven Lee and the University of New Hampshire, obtained through a right-to-know request. Jeremy Margolis is the Monitor's education reporter. He also covers the towns of Boscawen, Salisbury, and Webster, and the courts. This is essentially a reflection of how the covering analysts have collectively reassessed their initial estimates over this period. Investors should keep in mind that the direction of estimate revisions by each of the covering analysts may not always get reflected in the aggregate change. Estimate revisions ahead of a company's earnings release offer clues to the business conditions for the period whose results are coming out. This insight is at the core of our proprietary surprise prediction model -- the Zacks Earnings ESP (Expected Surprise Prediction). The Zacks Earnings ESP compares the Most Accurate Estimate to the Zacks Consensus Estimate for the quarter; the Most Accurate Estimate is a more recent version of the Zacks Consensus EPS estimate. The idea here is that analysts revising their estimates right before an earnings release have the latest information, which could potentially be more accurate than what they and others contributing to the consensus had predicted earlier. Thus, a positive or negative Earnings ESP reading theoretically indicates the likely deviation of the actual earnings from the consensus estimate. However, the model's predictive power is significant for positive ESP readings only. A positive Earnings ESP is a strong predictor of an earnings beat, particularly when combined with a Zacks Rank #1 (Strong Buy), 2 (Buy) or 3 (Hold). Our research shows that stocks with this combination produce a positive surprise nearly 70% of the time, and a solid Zacks Rank actually increases the predictive power of Earnings ESP. Please note that a negative Earnings ESP reading is not indicative of an earnings miss. Our research shows that it is difficult to predict an earnings beat with any degree of confidence for stocks with negative Earnings ESP readings and/or Zacks Rank of 4 (Sell) or 5 (Strong Sell). For UnitedHealth, the Most Accurate Estimate is higher than the Zacks Consensus Estimate, suggesting that analysts have recently become bullish on the company's earnings prospects. This has resulted in an Earnings ESP of +2.77%. On the other hand, the stock currently carries a Zacks Rank of #3. So, this combination indicates that UnitedHealth will most likely beat the consensus EPS estimate. While calculating estimates for a company's future earnings, analysts often consider to what extent it has been able to match past consensus estimates. So, it's worth taking a look at the surprise history for gauging its influence on the upcoming number. For the last reported quarter, it was expected that UnitedHealth would post earnings of $2.09 per share when it actually produced earnings of $2.11, delivering a surprise of +0.96%. Over the last four quarters, the company has beaten consensus EPS estimates two times. An earnings beat or miss may not be the sole basis for a stock moving higher or lower. Many stocks end up losing ground despite an earnings beat due to other factors that disappoint investors. Similarly, unforeseen catalysts help a number of stocks gain despite an earnings miss. That said, betting on stocks that are expected to beat earnings expectations does increase the odds of success. This is why it's worth checking a company's Earnings ESP and Zacks Rank ahead of its quarterly release. content: Read, Learn & Express – Discover the World with Swikblog UnitedHealth Group stock moved sharply higher, with UNH closing at $313.00, up 2.85%, as investors reacted to a stronger Medicare Advantage outlook and fresh analyst updates that improved sentiment around the healthcare giant. The move stands out as the stock continues to recover after a prolonged period of pressure. UnitedHealth remains one of the most closely watched healthcare names in the market and is often highlighted among top blue-chip stocks due to its scale and diversified business model spanning insurance and health services. Key trigger: A better-than-expected 2.48% Medicare Advantage rate increase, combined with analyst upgrades and price target hikes, drove renewed buying interest in UNH shares. The primary catalyst behind the rally was the finalized Medicare Advantage payment update. The 2.48% increase exceeded market expectations, which had been closer to a 1%–2% range. This provided improved visibility into 2027 reimbursement levels and helped ease concerns around profitability in managed care. For UnitedHealth, the update is particularly important given its strong exposure to Medicare Advantage plans through its insurance division. The improved rate outlook has helped investors reassess earnings stability after earlier concerns about rising medical costs. Wall Street quickly responded to the policy update. Bank of America raised its price target on UnitedHealth to $337 from $315, implying more than 10% upside from recent levels, while maintaining a ‘Neutral’ rating. The firm noted that improved reimbursement visibility across managed care companies is supporting valuation multiples. At the same time, Raymond James turned more optimistic on the stock, upgrading UnitedHealth from ‘Market Perform’ to ‘Outperform’. The shift highlights growing confidence among analysts that the worst of the recent pressure may be behind the company. UnitedHealth operates through two core segments that drive its long-term growth story. Its insurance arm, UnitedHealthcare, provides health benefits to millions of members, while Optum delivers healthcare services, pharmacy benefits, and data-driven solutions. This integrated structure has historically supported strong earnings growth and resilience, even during periods of market volatility. Investors continue to view the company as a dominant player in the healthcare ecosystem with multiple revenue streams. Despite the recent rally, the stock still trades well below its previous highs, keeping the valuation debate active. While improved policy clarity and analyst upgrades have strengthened sentiment, questions around cost trends and margin sustainability remain part of the broader investment discussion. Still, the latest move shows how quickly sentiment can shift when regulatory pressure eases. With stronger reimbursement visibility and renewed analyst support, UnitedHealth is once again drawing attention from investors looking for stability within large-cap healthcare stocks. title: Who is Rep. Sam Farrington? UNH student is behind campus guns bill - Seacoastonline.com
content: On March 2, New Hampshire state Rep. Sam Farrington, R-Rochester, and about a dozen supporters walked into a Durham Town Council meeting fully armed. They were there to protest the council’s resolution against Farrington’s campus carry bill, which would prohibit public colleges and universities from regulating guns on campuses. The council said the bill would make the University of New Hampshire and the surrounding community less safe. Farrington disagrees. “You are tyrants because you are stripping lawful adults of their individual rights,” Farrington said to the council. Farrington's bill to allow campus carry has ignited a firestorm around the state. The bill has garnered over 800 online testimony submissions, with 643 against and 231 in support, and students have spoken both for and against the bill in Concord. A UNH Student Senate Survey found 85% of students oppose the bill, and in early March students and faculty held a walkout in protest. And on April 11, the NHGOP added campus carry to their platform. The man behind it? Farrington, a sixth-generation resident of Rochester who is a UNH senior and a first-term state representative. Farrington, who is in his last semester at UNH, said he wanted to run for office to keep New Hampshire a haven for freedom. He said he's long been passionate about the state and wants to keep it from becoming like its neighboring states of Massachusetts and Maine. “There's a reason why people are leaving Massachusetts to come to New Hampshire. It's because of our state and our motto, live free or die, it's because freedom is maximized in New Hampshire, and government is limited to its core purpose,” Farrington said. “I'm really passionate about freedom, about our state. I want to keep it on the trajectory that it has been. And I think we have something really special here in New Hampshire.” As a junior in college in 2024, he defeated an incumbent Democrat to take office. Farrington said his top priority as a state legislator is defending the U.S. Constitution, which includes the Second Amendment right to carry arms. This year, he has sponsored five bills related to firearms, including bills to allow them to be carried by people on snowmobiles, OHRVs and slow-moving boats and, of course, the campus carry bill. At the Town Council meeting in Durham, Farrington argued individuals have a “natural right” to bear arms. “Natural rights transcend government,” Farrington said. “Natural rights come from God alone, and so what God gives we cannot take away.” A dissenter called his statement “ridiculous.” Farrington says he asks those who disagree with him to respect his constitutional rights. “I respect your First Amendment right to disagree with me, and I would ask you to respect my Second Amendment rights,” Farrington said. Farrington said he is a big proponent of free speech. It’s part of the reason he held a Q&A about his campus carry bill at UNH on March 10, where he challenged students to "Prove Sam wrong” in an event reminiscent of the “Prove Me Wrong” debate series the late conservative activist Charlie Kirk would hold at universities. Farrington said Kirk is someone he “absolutely” looks up to as a role model. Farrington helped organize a vigil for the Turning Point USA founder following his assassination. “Charlie's approach to politics was having a conversation with people that he disagreed with, and I think we need to return to that,” Farrington said. “I hosted that event because I wanted to talk to the students that disagreed with me.” Other political heroes for Farrington include Josiah Bartlett, a former governor of New Hampshire and signer of the Declaration of Independence, and U.S. Rep. Thomas Massie, R-Kentucky, a conservative lawmaker who has become known for pushing back against President Donald Trump, particularly regarding the Jeffrey Epstein files. Along with guns, Farrington’s priorities include a constitutional ban on income tax, supporting school choice and broader issues like the cost of living and housing. On social media, he has posted about other things he’d like to do, including lowering the drinking age and “seizing the Portsmouth Naval Shipyard and reclaiming it as New Hampshire property.” Farrington said those issues are not top priorities, and he tries “to bring humor to politics." Yet, most of his social media posts are serious, he said. The Portsmouth Naval Shipyard for example, he said, is a bipartisan issue. In February, the New Hampshire House voted for a resolution whose sponsors include Rep. Joseph Barton, R-Grafton, and Rep. Jennifer Mandelbaum, D-Portsmouth, to claim the shipyard lies solely within the Granite State and that “the state of New Hampshire holds absolute right and title to those lands submerged under the navigable waters of the whole of the Piscataqua River in trust for the people of the state.” Farrington's current term ends on Dec. 2, 2026. He wants to stay in politics, but he said he doesn't yet know what he'll be doing in the future. For now, he said, he likes the New Hampshire House of Representatives. Like all members of the House, his seat will be up for election this November. There are no official candidates yet for his district; the filing period is from June 3-12. As for the campus carry bill, it passed the House in February by a vote of 188-165. A Senate Judiciary hearing for the bill is set for April 14.

title: Huskies Host New Hampshire For Tuesday Night Tilt - University of Connecticut Athletics
content: STORRS, Conn. – The UConn women's lacrosse team will look to secure its third consecutive victory, returning home to face the UNH Wildcats on Tuesday evening at 6:00 pm. The Huskies are coming off a dominant 15-4 road win over Xavier, controlling the game from the opening draw. UConn jumped out to a 5-0 lead after the first quarter behind early goals from Emma Dacey, Hadley Bethke, Ava Palazzolo, Rayea Davis, and Anna Magennis. Bethke led the offensive surge with a four-goal performance, while Palazzolo notched the first goal of her collegiate career and added another in the second quarter. UConn maintained steady pressure throughout, extending its lead to 11-3 by the end of the third. Megan O'Brien paced the fourth quarter with a hat trick, and Madison Sweeney added her first goal of the season to cap off the scoring. The Huskies' balanced attack and strong defensive effort secured their most decisive win of the season heading into tomorrow's matchup. New Hampshire enters Tuesday's matchup following a loss to Harvard in its most recent contest. In the defeat, the Wildcats were led by Charlize Collins, who recorded a team-high three points on one goal and two assists while also adding three ground balls and three caused turnovers in an all-around performance. Makayla Keys and Libby Boland paced the scoring with two goals apiece, with Keys also putting a team-high three shots on goal. The Wildcats will look to build off those individual efforts as they prepare for a tough road test in Storrs. UConn has historically controlled the series, holding a 14-7 record against UNH, including a dominant 17-5 victory in the teams' last meeting in 2024. The Huskies will aim to continue that success, while the Wildcats look to reverse the trend on Tuesday.

title: College Athlete Profile: Porter Hurteau talks racing at UNH and what fuels him on and off the course - St. Albans Messenger
content:
Porter Hurteau, a graduate of BFA-St. Albans is racing in his sophomore year for the Division 1 University of New Hampshire Wildcats. Porter Hurteau, center, poses with his high school varsity coach, Michael Mashtare (right), who helped prepare him to compete at the Division 1 collegiate level. To Hurteau's left is Jason Storms, father of his former BFA-St. Albans' teammate Calvin Storms. Former BFA-St. Albans’ runners Jacob Tremblay, Ethan Mashtare, Porter Hurteau, and Teddy Tremblay are all running in for Division 1 colleges in the America East Conference. Porter Hurteau, a graduate of BFA-St. Albans is racing in his sophomore year for the Division 1 University of New Hampshire Wildcats. Porter Hurteau, a sophomore at the University of New Hampshire, studying chemical engineering, who’s also a three-season runner, began his running career at BFA-St. Albans, where he honed his skills, earning a spot with the Division 1 Wildcats. Why did you choose to study and run for UNH? For me, it was about finding somewhere that felt like home outside of Vermont. I grew up in a small town, and while I looked at the University of Vermont, I wanted an experience outside of the state. They have a great engineering program and a brand-new engineering building. I knew it was where I wanted to be. Why did you choose to study chemical engineering? Originally, I was going to go for mechanical engineering. I like to know the small details, and with chemical engineering, you’re working with the cellular level. It’s still a broad degree, and I’m getting a lot of opportunities and meeting a lot of new people in the industry. I really just wanted to try something new. Are you a car enthusiast? Yes, I like cars. In my junior year, I was sick of AP classes and decided to take automotive at Northwest Career and Technical Center, and it was super fun. I love working with vehicles—even every day cars. They’re machines, and you can figure them out with math. I love changing my own oil, headlights, and spark plugs. I love getting my hands dirty–compared to sitting in AP classes and listening to a lecture. My senior year, I took a class called Special Topics in Electric Aviation because it was taught by BETA Technologies. They were actual people from BETA who worked with Vermont State University. They took us through the simulators and through the class. I knew planes were cool, as were cars, and that’s how I got into chemical engineering. What is your goal for the use of your degree? I’d like to work with a performance division of an automotive company. You always have performance packages you can get, and I’d like to work on fuel systems and on making cars faster and more efficient. What do you enjoy about working with cars? I like math; I’ve been told my whole life I’m a good writer, but math has always been challenging for me, and I have to put time into learning it. I have a great memory, but I have to work at math and problem-solving. I like to sit down with math, work my way through it, and get the right answer. You know if you did it right, you did it right. If you didn’t, try again. There’s a sense of accomplishment for me when I’m sitting at a whiteboard and get the correct answer. What have you enjoyed about running for UNH? Honestly, I really enjoy being an athlete. So many people go to college to be students, and that’s why I’m here, but having something on campus that lets me escape the education side is awesome. I came to school and had a group of friends through sports. We spend at least two hours a day together, and we eat and spend time together. I love running and have done it competitively for seven years. It clears my head at the end of the day to go out and work hard with friends. We’re pushing our minds and bodies to the limit, and it’s awesome. Former BFA-St. Albans’ runners Jacob Tremblay, Ethan Mashtare, Porter Hurteau, and Teddy Tremblay are all running in for Division 1 colleges in the America East Conference. What are some memorable moments in cross country after two seasons? There have been a lot. My first race was at my home course at Hard’ack in St. Albans, VT, and that was pretty special. I got to go back to the course I’ve raced and trained on forever. In my first race, I had a breakthrough. I ran a great race—was fourth for my team and top 15. We were there a month later for another race. The day before the meet, I got off the bus and saw my high school teammate Calvin Storm’s mom walking the course. It was special to be back, and the people in the town are like family. Do you have memorable moments from your indoor track seasons? Last season, I redshirted for indoor track, so this was my first indoor season racing. On Saturday, Jan. 31, I was at Boston University. I finished the race and saw my former teammates, Jacob and Teddy Tremblay, cheering for me from the UVM section and telling me what I had just run. Lucas MacKenzie, another former teammate, was also there. What are you looking forward to for your outdoor track season? I’m looking forward to this outdoor season. The conference will be at the University of Massachusetts, Lowell, where my former teammate Ehtan Mashtare runs. I’m looking forward to racing with my old teammates again. Ethan Mashtare, Jacob, and Teddy Tremblay all run in that conference, and Teddy just got welcomed to the America East, since he’s racing at UVM for his freshman year. We won’t be on the same team, but it will feel like home because we’ve raced together for so long. I’m excited to prove myself and show others what all of the BFA-St. Albans grads can do. What does it mean to you to see so many BFA-St. Albans alumni are racing at a high level, and what does it say about the program? I feel a sense of pride. Coach Mike Mashtare built this legacy over the last 45 years he’s been coaching, and I think it’s a testament to his knowledge and willingness to work personally with his athletes. He created this environment for growth in a cross-country high school team. We trained as hard as we could and still had fun. It really shows he was dialed in his training, and he’s willing to work with you. The strength we’ve exhibited in the America East Conference demonstrates the coaches' knowledge at BFA. Even now, Coach Mashtare shows up at the conference meets and sees us all racing. He’ll comment on form, and we know we can trust him. He’s there on the sidelines, and he’ll always be there for us. Porter Hurteau, center, poses with his high school varsity coach, Michael Mashtare (right), who helped prepare him to compete at the Division 1 collegiate level. To Hurteau's left is Jason Storms, father of his former BFA-St. Albans' teammate Calvin Storms. Do you ever think about the Vermont connection while racing at Elle (Purrier) St. Pierre’s college? There’s definitely this feeling that her presence is always somewhere in the back of my mind. She’s a Vermonter, an Olympian, and a record holder. We saw her for the Hall of Fame induction last year. She’s ever-present, but the team and the coaches are focused on working with the athletes who are here in the present. What does it mean to you to grow up in a running family? Since we were young, we viewed our parents as role models. They’ve always been outdoors. We were sugaring in the spring, building huts in the woods, or going for family runs together. My dad has coached us up through high school and is coaching my brothers, Toby and Sam. It’s been nice to have that support. The first time I ran more than five miles was with my mother. I got dropped. We laugh looking back on it now that I’m a collegiate athlete. She’s always been there making granola bars to fuel us. My younger brothers have been an intrinsic motivator for me. I’ve wanted to lead by example, so I’ve always worked hard to be the best. Now that I’ve graduated from high school, it’s all about them and their last year of high school. We share our success and push each other. I’m proud of how hard they’ve worked and how our family has created this environment for us to realize our potential—so we’ve been able to do what we enjoy at the highest level. Do you have any wise words for younger athletes? Find something you love and are willing to put the work into. It’s hard to work hard for something you don’t want. If you have goals and want to reach them, work hard toward them. Try different things to reach those goals. It won’t be easy, but you’ll be willing to work a lot harder to achieve it. title: UNH's Grit to Grow helps teens explore careers, build leadership skills - Seacoastonline.com
content: Teens are invited to explore career opportunities and build leadership skills at the Grit to Grow Summit in the Lakes Region this June. The two-day event, hosted by the University of New Hampshire Extension in partnership with the Governor’s Task Force on Bring Back the Trades, will take place June 22-23, according to a community announcement. The summit aims to bring together teens, educators, employers, and community leaders to support young people as they consider life after high school. It will feature hands-on workshops, real-world career exploration, and discussions about college, trades, careers, and personal growth. “The saying, ‘It’s not just what you know, but who you know,’ is very relevant for our teens looking to determine their next steps along their career and education pathways,” says Kate Korbas, the state 4-H leader and youth development specialist, according to the announcement. The event will begin at Lakes Region Community College in Laconia, where teens will participate in interactive workshops focused on self-discovery, leadership, financial literacy, entrepreneurship, and storytelling. Sessions will cover topics such as paying bills, telling personal stories, and entrepreneurship. The second day will take place at the Inn at Mill Falls and is open to both overnight and one-day participants. Parents and caregivers are encouraged to attend alongside teens. During the second day, teens will engage in small group career exploration sessions with professionals from various industries, including skilled trades, healthcare, manufacturing, engineering, agriculture, hospitality, and government. Space is limited, and early registration is encouraged. The deadline to register is May 1. Scholarships are available for those seeking financial assistance. For registration and additional information, visit extension.unh.edu. This story was created by Kimberly Green, KGreen@usatodayco.com, with the assistance of Artificial Intelligence (AI). title: Ski industry workers are worried about climate change, UNH research shows - New Hampshire Public Radio
content:
New research from the University of New Hampshire shows professionals in the state’s ski industry are particularly worried about climate change. In a survey given last summer, 85% of New Hampshire ski professionals said they were either “alarmed” or “concerned” about climate change. Those results are much higher than the national average: 54%. More than half of the survey respondents did not think their industry in New Hampshire was doing enough to address global warming. The researchers said their results may point to motivation among ski industry workers to implement more sustainable practices and push for policy that could help limit the future impacts of climate change. “My hope is that the skiing community in New Hampshire recognizes that many of us share similar concerns about climate change,” researcher Elizabeth Burakowski said in a press release. “And, that we can collectively take action to reduce fossil fueled greenhouse gas emissions, use and produce energy responsibly and efficiently, and strategize practices that protect our winters while sustaining our outdoor recreational economy.” Get daily top stories from NHPR's newsroom with The Rundown. 
                        Check out all of NHPR's newsletters here. New Hampshire’s ski industry generates roughly $278.8 million each ski season, and supports 10,000 seasonal and 6,000 annual jobs, according to the researchers. Ski hills have tried to adapt, investing in snowmaking systems and branching into warm-weather activities like ziplining and biking. This winter was cold and snowy. But over time the ski season is shrinking, as New England winters warm. A statewide climate assessment from 2021 projected New Hampshire could see up to 50% less snow by 2099. The state averages around 100 days of deep natural snowpack sufficient for skiing each year. But, the researchers said, by the end of the century that could shrink to 60 or even 30 days, depending on future fossil fuel emissions. NHPR is nonprofit and independent. title: UnitedHealth (UNH) Stock Soars 8% on Medicare Advantage Rate Increase – Can Momentum Last? - mexc.co
content: UnitedHealth Group (UNH) experienced its strongest weekly performance in seven months, surging more than 8% following an unexpected Medicare Advantage payment increase from federal regulators. The Centers for Medicare & Medicaid Services confirmed a 2.48% boost to Medicare Advantage reimbursement rates for 2027. This represented a substantial upgrade from preliminary guidance that had suggested rates would remain virtually unchanged. The news triggered an impressive single-day rally approaching double digits. Market participants had been positioning for disappointing news, making the elevated rate an unexpected positive catalyst. Medicare Advantage has served as a critical revenue driver for UNH throughout the past ten years. However, escalating healthcare expenses combined with constrained government reimbursements had begun compressing profitability, making this regulatory decision particularly significant. Bernstein analysts responded swiftly following the announcement. The research firm elevated its UNH price objective to $411 while reaffirming its Buy stance. According to Bernstein’s analysis, the CMS ruling transforms what appeared to be a potential 4% drag on 2027 profits into anticipated earnings expansion of approximately 1.4%. That represents a substantial turnaround. On the same trading day, HSBC analyst Sidharth Sahoo elevated UNH to a Hold recommendation. While stopping short of a bullish call, the upgrade signals a recalibration of downside risks. The enthusiasm isn’t universal. Michael Ha from Baird maintained his Underperform stance, positioning him as the sole bearish voice among 31 sell-side analysts tracking the healthcare giant. Ha contends the payment bump may provide only short-term relief. His analysis highlights that fundamental challenges facing value-based care frameworks remain unresolved. That perspective deserves consideration. The Medicare Advantage segment continues facing a complex operating landscape, despite the favorable rate adjustment. UNH recently forecasted declining 2026 revenues — potentially marking its first annual sales decline in more than thirty years. Membership levels are projected to decrease across commercial, Medicare, and Medicaid segments. Shares remain down roughly 7% year-to-date and trade over 50% beneath their 2024 peak. This week’s advance narrows those losses, but significant ground remains to be recovered. Nevertheless, the consensus view on Wall Street leans positive. Among 31 analysts following UNH, 22 maintain Buy recommendations. The consensus 12-month price target implies approximately 17% potential upside from current trading levels. Optum, UNH’s healthcare services division encompassing pharmacy benefit management and care delivery operations, continues offering earnings stability while the traditional insurance segment navigates obstacles. Market participants are now focused on Q1 2026 results. Healthcare cost trends and any revised Medicare Advantage profitability outlook will be critical focal points. title: UnitedHealth (UNH) Stock Jumps 8% — Is the Medicare Relief Rally the Real Deal? - mexc.co
content: UnitedHealth Group (UNH) had its best week in seven months, climbing over 8% after the US government delivered a surprise Medicare Advantage payment boost. The Centers for Medicare & Medicaid Services finalized a 2.48% increase in 2027 Medicare Advantage reimbursement rates. That was a sharp improvement from an earlier proposal that had rates staying nearly flat. The announcement sparked a near double-digit rally in a single trading session. Investors had been bracing for the worst, so the higher rate came as a genuine relief. Medicare Advantage has been a core growth engine for UNH for over a decade. But rising medical costs and tighter government funding had started squeezing margins, making this ruling a much-needed development. Bernstein moved quickly after the news. The firm raised its price target on UNH to $411 and kept its Buy rating in place. Analyst commentary from Bernstein suggested the CMS decision flips what could have been a roughly 4% hit to 2027 earnings into expected earnings growth of around 1.4%. That’s a meaningful swing. HSBC analyst Sidharth Sahoo upgraded UNH to Hold on the same day. It was a measured move — not a full endorsement, but a sign that the risk profile had shifted. Not everyone is buying the optimism.
